did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes a pilot study using AI teledermatology software (a UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device) to triage skin cancer referrals. The AI immediately assessed dermoscopic images of lesions to identify benign cases for discharge back to GPs, while non-benign cases were triaged by dermatologists. The study reports outcomes such as a 34% overall discharge rate and a 62% reduction in urgent face-to-face appointments.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The text is an abstract for a pilot study (BT06) reporting original experimental results from a clinical trial using AI teledermatology software to triage skin cancer referrals. It details specific methodology (four community-based hubs, UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device) and reports quantitative outcomes such as a 34% overall discharge rate and a 62% reduction in urgent face-to-face appointments.

title: BT06 Using artificial intelligence to triage skin cancer referrals: outcomes from a pilot study
brief_summary: This pilot study evaluated the use of AI teledermatology software, a UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device, to triage skin cancer referrals at four community-based hubs. The system assessed dermoscopic images to identify benign cases for discharge back to general practitioners, while non-benign cases were triaged by dermatologists. Over the study period, the AI identified 19% of cases as benign, resulting in an overall discharge rate of 34% after dermatologist safety checks. The implementation of this AI pathway led to a 62% reduction in urgent face-to-face appointments with dermatologists. Additionally, 38% of patients still required urgent appointments, while 13% were triaged to routine pathways. The study demonstrates that AI-assisted triage can streamline primary care referrals and reduce unnecessary travel for patients.

lesion_types_studied.details: Cases identified as benign by the AI were discharged back to the GP with benign diagnosis/lesion-reassurance. 19% of cases were discharged with a benign diagnosis.
Cases suspected to be non-benign were triaged by dermatologists. This category includes specific subtypes mentioned below.
Specifically 'Low-risk basal cell carcinomas' were mentioned as a subset of non-benign cases that could be discharged back to the GP with management advice.
Patients with precancerous lesions were discharged back to the GP with management advice.

The study focuses on triaging 'skin cancer referrals'. While specific malignant types like melanoma are not explicitly listed as separate extraction targets in the provided text, the context implies the study covers suspected skin cancers, with specific mentions of BCC and precancerous lesions.

all_task_types.details: The AI teledermatology software assesses dermoscopic images to identify benign cases for discharge. The system performs binary classification (benign vs. non-benign) to triage referrals, with 19% of cases identified as benign by the AI.

datasets_used.name: Pilot Study Cohort (BT06)
datasets_used.description: A cohort of patients presenting with suspected skin cancer referrals at four community-based photography hubs (including Stirling Community Hospital, University Hospitals of Leicester, Hetherington Group Practice, and Skin Analytics) participating in a pilot study of AI teledermatology software. The study started in March 2022 and processes 5-600 cases per month.

use.testing: The cohort was used to evaluate the outcomes of the AI teledermatology software in a clinical setting, specifically measuring discharge rates and reduction in urgent appointments.
use.other: The anonymized images and metadata from this cohort are transferred to a nationwide database for deep learning AI algorithm development.

datasets_used.name: Nationwide Database
datasets_used.description: A nationwide database where anonymized images and metadata compliant with DICOM standards are securely transferred from the pilot study hubs.

use.training: Used for deep learning AI algorithm development.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Percentage of patients receiving telephone call for biopsy consent
Percentage of patients referred directly to another speciality
Percentage of cases triaged to routine pathway
Percentage of patients requiring urgent face-to-face appointment

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper is a pilot study abstract reporting descriptive outcomes such as discharge rates and reduction in appointments without statistical significance testing.

metric: Percentage of patients receiving telephone call for biopsy consent
Percentage of patients referred directly to another speciality
Percentage of cases triaged to routine pathway
Percentage of patients requiring urgent face-to-face appointment
Pilot Study Cohort (BT06).notes: The study processed 5-600 cases per month at four community-based hubs starting in March 2022. The AI identified 19% of cases as benign. Trust dermatologists discharged an additional 15% of cases, resulting in a total discharge rate of 34%. The implementation led to a 62% reduction in urgent face-to-face appointments. The trust saw >90% 2-week wait referrals within 2 weeks in the last two quarters prior to the AI introduction.

architecture_used.main_model: AI teledermatology software (UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device)

architecture_details.description: The paper describes the system as 'AI teledermatology software' and a 'UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device'. It utilizes deep learning algorithms developed from a nationwide database of anonymized images and metadata compliant with DICOM standards. The specific model architecture (e.g., CNN, transformer, backbone) is not reported.
architecture_details.deployment: Deployed at four community-based photography hubs for triaging skin cancer referrals.

training_details.training_data_source: Nationwide database of anonymized images and metadata compliant with DICOM standards.

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (benign vs. non-benign) for triage purposes.
classification_or_output_head.output: Discharge back to GP (benign), urgent appointment, routine appointment, or direct to biopsy/speciality.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AI teledermatology software (UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device)
preprocessing_techniques: Anonymization of images and metadata before transfer to the nationwide database for deep learning AI algorithm development
Conversion of datasets into DICOM format for AI research
Standardization of capture, transfer, and storage of skin images and clinical data based on DICOM standards
conclusions: AI teledermatology software (a UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device) can effectively triage skin cancer referrals in a community-based setting, identifying 19% of cases as benign for discharge back to GPs.

The implementation of AI-assisted triage resulted in an overall discharge rate of 34% (including 15% discharged by trust dermatologists) and a 62% reduction in urgent face-to-face appointments with dermatologists.
The AI pathway streamlined primary care referrals, with 13% of cases triaged to routine pathways and 38% still requiring urgent face-to-face appointments.
The study demonstrates that AI can reduce unnecessary travel for patients and alleviate pressure on dermatology services, particularly in the context of increased demand.
Ongoing audit is required to evaluate the safety, acceptability, and cost-effectiveness of this AI triage pathway to inform future decision-making locally and across the UK.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study abstract reporting descriptive outcomes (discharge rates, appointment reductions) without statistical significance testing or p-values.

The AI system is a UKCA marked Class IIa Medical Device, regulated by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency, indicating it is a validated clinical tool rather than a research prototype.
The study lacks external validation; it was conducted at four specific community-based hubs (Stirling, Leicester, London) and results may not generalize to other settings.
There is a potential for selection bias as 'appropriate cases' were selected for teledermatology appointments, which may not represent the full spectrum of primary care referrals.

The AI's benign discharge was contingent on a safety check by a consultant dermatologist employed by the AI provider, meaning the reported 19% benign rate is not solely an AI decision but a human-AI collaborative outcome.
The study does not report the total number of patients included, only the monthly volume (5-600 cases), limiting the assessment of statistical power.
Ongoing audit is explicitly noted as required to evaluate safety, acceptability, and cost-effectiveness, indicating that long-term clinical safety data is not yet available.
The study addresses a specific context of high demand (30% increase in skin cancer clinic demand) and may not reflect performance in lower-volume or different healthcare systems.
